text:
High concentrations of particulate matter (PM) is a major public health issue and for decision making accurate and reliable data is required. This report gives an overview of the results of the second Netherlands Research Program on Particulate Matter, BOP II (2010–2012), carried out by RIVM, TNO and ECN as a continuation of the first BOP study. The aim of BOP II was to eliminate the scientific uncertainties associated with measuring and modelling the PM components, i.e. secondary inorganic aerosols (SIA), elemental and organic carbon (EC/OC) and source apportionment.

This release reports key findings concerning the sulphur, iodine and arsenic (SIA) species; correction factors for which had been derived by BOP II in order to establish a solid basis for a new sampling technique. Furthermore, the findings concerning the nitrogen and sulfur compounds composition revealed that their contribution to the PM mass is greater than previously thought. High resolution modelling and monitoring activities carried out in Rotterdam demonstrated that the concentration of primary PM components (such as elemental carbon and heavy metals due to brake and tyre wear) in urban environments is largely driven by the background conditions and that in general these primary components are 2-3 times higher in the hotspots caused by local traffic. Moreover, the radiocarbon analysis indicated that the organic carbon (OC) source apportionment has been: - 70% biogenic and biomass burning - 30% fossil sources

About one-third of the man-made PM produced in the Netherlands is created domestically (e.g. by agriculture and traffic), while about two-thirds comes from abroad. This study shows that the reduction of local air pollution caused by traffic will not suffice, and that there must be a connection to international policies to protect people's health from fine particles in the air.
